coredata.dc:title: Magnetic and luminescent neodymium-doped carbon dot–cyclodextrin polymer nanocomposite as an anticancer drug-carrier
coredata.dc:creator: Alexander, Aleyamma
Sumohan Pillai, Archana
Manikantan, Varnitha
Sri Varalakshmi, Govindaraj
Allben Akash, Bose
Enoch, Israel V.M.V.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a neodymium-doped carbon dot composite with a poly-β-cyclodextrin. It possesses wavelength-dependent photoluminescence characteristics. In addition, the material displays magnetic characteristics due to the doped neodymium element. The nanocomposite is soluble in water and accommodates the anticancer drug, camptothecin. The release of the loaded drug is pH-dependent. Further, the anticancer activity of the free- and the drug-loaded carrier are studied on MCF-7 cells. The biological activity and the luminescence and magnetic properties of the nanocarrier are demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanocompsites
Magnetic materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Neodymium doped carbon dots
Luminescence and magnetic characteristic of the NCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the DAE)–BRNS, Government of India, for the project: 37(2)/14/17/2018-BRNS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. X-ray diffraction pattern of the as synthesized C-dot : Nd and the NC. B. FT-IR spectra of the as-synthesized C-dot : Nd and the NC. C. TEM image of the NC. D. Particle size distribution as shown by dynamic light scattering. E. Zeta potential of the NC.

A. Absorption spectrum of Poly-CD-C-dot : Nd NCs. Inset: Absorption spectrum of as-synthesized C-dots. B. Images of the solutions of NCs at dark and on illumination at 430 nm. C. Excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence spectra of NCs. D. Magnetization vs. field curves of the as-synthesized C-dots : Nd and the NC.

Cytotoxic effects of A. NC and B. CPT-loaded NC on MCF-7 cells. Values are expressed as mean ± SD for three experiments. Values that do not share a common superscript letter are significantly different at p < 0.05 by one-way ANOVA followed by DMRT. Micrographs displaying the morphological changes on treatment with C. free- and D. CPT- loaded NCs. Control: Live cells in DMEM medium. The drug loading content on the NCs is 3.1 ± 0.4. The entrapment efficiency is 86.3%.

head.ce:title: Magnetic and luminescent neodymium-doped carbon dot–cyclodextrin polymer nanocomposite as an anticancer drug-carrier

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences (Deemed-to-be University), Coimbatore 641114, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Centre for Nanoscience and Genomics, Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences (Deemed-to-be University), Coimbatore 641114, Tamil Nadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Karunya Institute of Technology and Sciences (Deemed-to-be University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a neodymium-doped carbon dot composite with a poly-β-cyclodextrin. It possesses wavelength-dependent photoluminescence characteristics. In addition, the material displays magnetic characteristics due to the doped neodymium element. The nanocomposite is soluble in water and accommodates the anticancer drug, camptothecin. The release of the loaded drug is pH-dependent. Further, the anticancer activity of the free- and the drug-loaded carrier are studied on MCF-7 cells. The biological activity and the luminescence and magnetic properties of the nanocarrier are demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Several nanocarriers have entered various phases of clinical trials as anticancer therapeutic transporters [1]. Needless to say, it is highly intriguing to engineer nanocarriers with multiple functionalities and explore their anticancer drug-carrying capacity because cancer remains as a difficult-to-cure chronic disease [2]. Breast cancer is a widespread form of cancer, frequently diagnosed among patients [2]. The efficacy of drugs is limited owing to the poor availability of the drugs at the target site [3]. Therefore, development of newer NC is desirable for transporting anticancer drugs. Carbon dots (C-dots) are carbon nanoparticles possessing intriguing luminescence characteristics. A step-forward in the design of C-dots is doping of various elements in them. Such doped C-dots find application in various fields, especially in bioimaging [4] and directed drug delivery [5].

Neodymium, is an element that usually imparts interesting luminescence and magnetic characteristics to materials and organometallic compounds they form. Therefore, it is fascinating to explore Nd-doped C-dots. CDs possess a hydrophobic cavity and a hydrophilic outer surface [6]. CD allows a sustained release of the encapsulated drug [7]. They are employed as nanocarriers of anticancer therapeutics and are traceable both by luminescence imaging and MRI. Herein, we report a nanocomposite (NC) of a β-Cyclodextrin (CD)-oligomer with Nd-doped C-dots.

The materials used viz., Neodymium nitrate and o-phenyldiamine were obtained from Fischer Scientific (India). β-Cyclodextrin and epichlorohydrin were obtained from Alfa Aesar (India). The chemicals had 98% purity (AR).

The polymer (Poly-CD) was synthesized according to the procedure described in Koopmans et. al. [8] by treating NaOH solution with β-CD at 35 °C for 2 h and then adding toluene and epichlorohydrin. The product was dialyzed against water using a dialysis bag (MW CO: 12–14 kDa) and then lyophilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Neodymium doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Neodymium doped carbon dots, coated with poly CD was prepared by hydrothermal method. 0.5 g of o-phenyl diamine and 0.1 g of NdNO3·6H2O were dissolved in 50 ml distilled water. 0.2 g of poly-CD was mixed with the above solution and placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The temperature was maintained at 180 °C for 5 h. The nanoparticles were precipitated and dried at room temperature.
The in vitro drug release measurements, in vitro studies on cell lines, and instrumentation are discussed in the Supporting Information SI 1.

The poly-CD-Nd-doped nanocomposites (NCs) display an XRD pattern as shown in Fig. 1 A. The peaks are large in number and characteristic of an amorphous nature of the C-dots. Peaks are observed at 2θ degree 20.7, 21.9, 26.2, 26.8, 31.5, 34.0, 36.1, and 45.3. The most prominent graphitic (002) plane is observed at 20.7° [9]. The peaks at 26.2, 26.8, 31.5, and 45.3 conform to the reported 2θ values for C-dots without dopants with slight shifts. The additional peaks above 45.3° arise owing to the substituted Nd. Peaks associated with oxidized Nd are not observed.

In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 B), the as-synthesized C-dots display bands at 3366.66 (broad), 1626.96, and 1525.48 cm−1 corresponding to the O–H str., CO str., and N–H bending of the OC-NH2. The 1383.86 cm-1band corresponds to O–H bending. It implies the presence of O–H and not COOH since the latter would result in bands above 1395 cm−1 [10]. Bands of the NCs at 2928.04 and 2956.94 cm−1 arise due to the β-CD aliphatic C–H str. and O–H band, and the 1077.49 and 1029.63 cm-1bands due to the C-O str. of the poly-CD.

The TEM micrographs (Fig. 1 C) show C-dots of size ∼20 nm and can be viewed as being inside the NCs (144 ± 8 nm). The narrow size distribution of the NCs was further revealed by DLS (Fig. 1 D). The NCs showed a mean zeta potential value (Fig. 1 E) of 85 (area 100%), suggesting the colloidal stability of the NCs [11]. The samples were investigated employing EDX, XPS, and TGA (see SI 2 and 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence and magnetic characteristic of the NCs
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis bands at 262 and 430 nm depict the characteristic absorption of the C-dots [12], i.e., n-π* transition of CC, n-π* transition of CO/C-OH electrons (430 nm) [13] and the π–π* transitions (262 nm). Unlike reported C-dots [14], the currently synthesized NCs show longer wavelength absorptions, due to the formation of NCs that extend the conjugation. An aqueous solution of the NCs is yellow and under an illuminating light of 430 nm, it shows a bright green color (Fig. 2 B).

The PL spectrum (Fig. 2 C) of the aqueous solution of NC appears at 560 nm (λex = 430 nm). Further, it is dependent on the excitation wavelength. A shoulder at 660 nm is observed when excited at 480 nm. Nd in organometallic complexes has shown such a band [15]. Contrarily, the percentage of Nd in the C-dots is just 5% and therefore the band is not seen distinct. Nevertheless, the emission at 630 nm becomes prominent at excitations above 500 nm, due to the charge-transferring influence of Nd. In addition, the f–f transition is also weak. In summary, the NC displayed a tunable emission that is advantageous for their application in cellular imaging.

The as-synthesized C-dots show a sigmoidal magnetization curve (Fig. 2 D) with a small coercivity of 37.28 Oe. The saturation magnetization (MS) is 0.50 emu g−1. The coercivity (37.28 Oe) is smaller than reported for Nd-compounds [16]. The C-dots are superparamagnetic. They NPs respond rapidly to the magnetic field [17]. The magnetic property originates from the doped Nd3+ in the C-dots [18]. The magnetization of the NC, is weak because of the diamagnetic contribution of the poly-CD. The NC shows a paramagnetic behavior as shown in the Fig. 3 D. Such paramagnetic nanomaterials have been employed in MRI [19].

The release of the drug from the NCs was sustained and dependent on the pH of the medium (SI 4). The in vitro anticancer activity of the NCs was investigated on breast cancer (MCF-7) cell lines. The cytotoxicity (Fig. 3 A and B) is dose-dependent. The IC50 of free NCs is 20 μg/mL. When CPT is loaded on the NC, the IC50 improves to 10 μg/mL. 10 μg/mL of CPT-loaded NC contains 5 μg of CPT, implying that its efficacy is comparable to that of free-CPT [20,21], assuming the predominant activity of the drug on comparison to the NC, thus neglecting the activity of the latter in the combination. Nevertheless, at 24 h, the released amount of CPT from the NC is small and therefore, the efficacy is enhanced when loaded on the NC. Fig. 3 C and D show the morphological changes on treatment with the free- and drug-loaded NCs respectively. The MCF-7 cell lines reveal shrinkage, detachment, membrane-blebbing, and distortion of shape. Furthermore, the apoptosis induced by CPT and the CPT-loaded nanocarrier were 56.61% for CPT and 52.16% for CPT-loaded nanocarrier at IC50 concentrations of 2.09 and 10 μg/mL, respectively (See SI 5).

Neodymium-doped C-dots are synthesized. They form a composite with a β-CD polymer. The nanocomposite is luminescent and paramagnetic. The release of the loaded CPT is sustained and pH-dependent. The anticancer efficacy of the CPT on MCF-7 cells is improved when loaded on the CPT-loaded NC. The NC is suitable as an anticancer drug delivery vehicle and as a traceable nanomaterial though luminescence/MRI imaging. In addition, as the poly-β-CD carries cavity-containing cyclodextrin moieties, the study can be extended for the transport of other drugs also.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the DAE)–BRNS, Government of India, for the project: 37(2)/14/17/2018-BRNS.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.131830.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
